narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80.5% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your third-year grade decile, which ranked in the bottom tier and stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction—far below the mid-to-upper deciles typical among students who passed the bar. Your first-year grade decile, also at the lowest level compared to the much higher averages for successful bar takers, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element driving the risk higher. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA provided a slight counterbalance, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it was closer to the averages seen in passers, though not enough to offset the other weaknesses. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who passed—where it's the most common bracket—and your plan to attend full-time matches the vast majority of successful students, these didn't carry enough weight to change the outcome. Your LSAT score of 31 also fell notably short of the 37 average for bar passers, adding to the overall picture of elevated risk. Compared to typical admitted students, who generally show stronger early and later undergrad performance, your profile signals a tougher path to bar success in the eyes of the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven flag on academic indicators that have historically predicted bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward 37 or higher, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and growth since those early low deciles. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools like this one. You're clearly determined, and with targeted improvements, the data suggests a brighter path ahead.
